name: valerian root
mechanism: Valerenic-acid-containing fractions may positively modulate GABA-A receptor signaling.
interactions: May increase sedation when combined with CNS depressants
Potential additive CNS depression with benzodiazepines
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated. May cause daytime drowsiness, headache or gastrointestinal discomfort. Avoid combining with alcohol or sedative-hypnotics without medical guidance; Avoid during pregnancy because safety evidence is limited; Use caution before driving or operating machinery due to drowsiness risk; Avoid combining with sedatives, generally well tolerated; Avoid combining with alcohol or sedative-hypnotics without medical guidance, daytime drowsiness, headache or gastrointestinal discomfort; Avoid combining with sedatives.

sources.title: PubMed search: valerian root
sources.note: Literature index for pharmacology and safety evidence

description: Valerian root is usually selected for bedtime-focused routines when users want a stronger evening herb than tea-only calming options.
dosage: Common use for sleep is 300-600 mg standardized extract 30-120 minutes before bedtime.
duration: Commonly used in short sleep-support blocks or intermittent bedtime use based on next-day grogginess tolerance.
therapeuticUses: Sleep routine escalation after tea-first strategies feel too light
Night-focused calm support when daytime sedation is acceptable
categoryUseContext: Primarily compared for sleep-onset support, nighttime wind-down, and sedative-leaning herbal protocols.
evidenceLevel: Mixed-to-moderate evidence for sleep-related outcomes with notable preparation variability across products.
